hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Arriving and Settling In -- Airports, Hotels, and the First Hours in a New City
lesson.menu_desc: Landing at JFK, going through customs and checking into a hotel in New York -- 38 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- Arriving and Settling In
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Aula 2 -- Arriving and Settling In

listenings.text: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to New York. We have just landed at John F. Kennedy International Airport, Terminal Four, and the local time is nine fifteen in the evening. Please remain seated with your seat belt fastened until the aircraft has come to a complete stop. If you have a connecting flight tonight, the transfer desk is on the upper level, and an agent will help you find your gate. If New York is your final destination, follow the signs to passport control, and then to baggage claim. Your checked bags will arrive on belt six. Please do not leave your carry-on in the overhead bin. On behalf of the crew, thank you for flying with us, and enjoy your stay in the city.

Hi, this is a message from the front desk. I am calling about your reservation for tonight. I have good news and one small problem. The good news first. We have already received your confirmation number, so your check-in is quick, and the concierge has already booked a table for you tomorrow at the Italian restaurant on the corner. Now the small problem. Your room is on the eighth floor, and the cleaning team has not finished it yet, so it will not be ready until three o'clock. If you arrive before that, please leave your bags with us and have a coffee in the lobby. Everything else is good to go. Call the front desk if you need anything at all. See you soon.

paras: The plane lands at JFK at nine fifteen in the evening. Lucimara has been awake for eleven hours, and the only thing she wants is a bed. But the first ninety minutes in a new country never belong to you: they belong to the airport.

First, passport control. The line is long, and the officer speaks quickly. "Business or pleasure?" "Business," she says. "How long are you staying?" "Five days." Two questions, two answers -- and she is in. Then baggage claim, belt six. Her suitcase has already arrived; her carry-on has never left her shoulder. She has not gone through customs yet, but she has nothing to declare, so the green line takes four minutes.

Outside, the taxi line moves fast. She gives the driver the address of the hotel, and she sends one message to her husband: "I've just landed. I'm on my way to the hotel." Forty minutes later, the front desk. She has already completed the online check-in, so the clerk only needs her confirmation number and her passport. There is one small problem: the room is not ready yet. It is nine at night, and the room is not ready. She could be angry. Instead, she leaves her bags at the front desk, asks the concierge for a place to eat nearby, and orders a bowl of soup two blocks away.

At eleven, she is finally in her room. She has not unpacked yet, and she will not unpack tonight. The jet lag will hit her at four in the morning, and she knows it. But the itinerary says the trade fair starts at nine, and she is good to go.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Arriving in a new city is a sequence of small steps, and each one has its own language.
New York airports are the worst airports in the world.
Business travelers should never take a taxi from the airport.

items: Her suitcase was already at baggage claim when she arrived.
"Her suitcase has already arrived" -- already = it happened before she expected it.
She went through customs before passport control.
"She has not gone through customs yet" -- customs comes after baggage claim.
She completed the online check-in before the trip.
"She has already completed the online check-in" -- so the clerk only needs the confirmation number.
The hotel room was ready when she arrived.
"The room is not ready yet" -- yet = we are still waiting for it.
She unpacked her suitcase before going to sleep.
"She has not unpacked yet, and she will not unpack tonight."

items.situation: You reach the front desk after a long flight. Start the check-in.
tips: Good evening. I have a reservation under the name Ito.
I've already completed the online check-in.

items.situation: The clerk asks: "Do you have your confirmation number?"
tips: Yes, I have it here. It's L-M-I, four, seven, two.
Spell it slowly -- letters are the hardest part on the phone.

items.situation: The clerk says your room is not ready. It is nine at night and you are exhausted. React.
tips: I understand. Could I leave my bags here until the room is ready?
Could you call me at the lobby when it's ready?

items.situation: You want to eat, but you don't know the area. Ask the concierge.
tips: Could you recommend somewhere to eat near the hotel?
Is there anywhere still open at this hour?

items.situation: Your suitcase is not on the belt and everybody else has already left. Ask for help.
tips: Excuse me, my bag hasn't arrived yet. Who can I talk to?
I've already waited twenty minutes at belt six.

items.situation: The officer at passport control speaks fast and you miss the question.
tips: Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat it, please?
Do you mean how long I'm staying?
